purpose: Craft examples for underworld, ghost-service, strange inn, supernatural business, and rule-bound liminal-space stories.
source_priority_rules: The source decides what the underworld rules are; this pack must not invent judge systems, punishments, creatures, or rituals.
Service roles, menus, ledgers, counters, doors, trays, lamps, and thresholds are useful only when source or locked payload supports them.
Use supernatural pressure through visible rule change, service ritual, customer arrival, spatial contradiction, or accounting consequence.
genre_boundary: Focus on rules, transactions, taboos, debts, service posture, and threshold spaces.
Do not turn every beat into combat; pressure can come from etiquette, account records, guest identity, or a changed room.
Keep the strange business logic causal: order -> rule -> price -> consequence.

scene_card_examples.visual: At the locked business counter, the protagonist sees a source-grounded ledger or menu change by itself while the service character waits without moving.
scene_card_examples.voiceover: The first rule is not spoken by a person; it appears in the business record.

asset_goal.purpose: counter, rule object, protagonist reaction, service character posture

scene_card_examples.visual: A threshold shot shows the safe interior and the unsafe outside existing in the same frame, with characters holding their exact locked positions.
scene_card_examples.voiceover: The door is not an exit anymore; it is a boundary with a price.

script_scene_examples.visual: The protagonist checks a source-grounded object at the counter; the service character confirms only what the source allows.
script_scene_examples.voiceover: He realizes the shop is operating before the first customer even appears.

dialogue.speaker: service character
dialogue.line: It changed when the guest was counted.

script_scene_examples.action_result: The rule object proves a guest or debt is already active.

dialogue_style_examples: The protagonist speaks in short operational questions or commands when trying to regain control.
Service characters should sound precise, restrained, and rule-bound, not emotionally explanatory.
Investigator roles should name evidence and readings, not explain the entire mythology.

negative_examples.bad: A random handkerchief appears as a clue in the underworld lobby.
negative_examples.why_bad: Unsupported props create false clue logic and confuse image/video continuity.

negative_examples.bad: Characters run toward a monster without a stated goal.
negative_examples.why_bad: It turns rule pressure into causeless action and breaks story logic.

forbidden_drift: Do not add new underworld officials, monsters, punishments, contracts, or ritual tools unless source-grounded.
Do not change a service worker into a combat role unless the source supports it.
Do not move a threat across a door, counter, or threshold without a visible transition or rule reason.
Do not rewrite relationship status, worker identity, public/private knowledge state, or ownership of the room's rules without source support.

image: Lock counter, ledger/menu, service posture, threshold, and lighting as continuity anchors.
video: Use doors, trays, ledgers, bells, table settings, and empty hall shots as bridge cutaways only when source-grounded.
